title: Notification System
prompt: Design a notification system that can send notifications to users via multiple channels: push notifications (iOS/Android), SMS, email, and in-app. The system should support different notification types, user preferences, rate limiting, and template management.

functional: Send notifications via push, SMS, email, and in-app channels
User notification preferences (opt-in/opt-out per channel and type)
Scheduled notifications (send at a specific time)
nonFunctional: High throughput (millions of notifications per day)
Reliable delivery with retry and DLQ
No duplicate notifications to the same user for same event
Graceful degradation when a channel provider is down

keyQuestions: Do users control their notification preferences?
Do we need rate limiting to prevent notification fatigue?
What are the latency requirements per priority level?

keyCalculations: 10K events/sec, average fan-out to 10 channels/users = 100K deliveries/sec
SMS: most expensive, rate limited by providers to ~1K/sec

expectedEndpoints: POST /api/v1/notifications/send { userId/userIds/topic, templateId, data, channels[], priority, scheduledAt? }
PUT /api/v1/users/{id}/preferences { email: bool, push: bool, sms: bool, ... }

expectedEntities: notifications: id, user_id, template_id, channel, status, priority, created_at, sent_at, read_at
templates: id, name, channel, subject_template, body_template, variables[]

expectedComponents: Channel Workers (email worker, push worker, SMS worker, in-app worker)

suggestedTopics: Reliability: retry strategy, DLQ, idempotency keys
Rate limiting per user to prevent notification fatigue
Priority queue implementation for urgent notifications
Provider failover: FCM down => fallback to APNs or SMS
Deduplication: exactly-once delivery despite at-least-once infrastructure
Batch notifications: grouping multiple events into digest

checklist: Asked about user preferences and opt-out
Discussed priority levels and latency requirements

anchors.1: Started designing without understanding channel requirements or scale
anchors.2: Asked about channels but missed preferences, priorities, or reliability
anchors.3: Covered channels, preferences, priorities, and reliability requirements
anchors.4: Proactively identified deduplication, rate limiting, provider failover, and digest scenarios

anchors.1: Direct synchronous calls to each channel provider, no queuing
anchors.2: Single queue with workers but no per-channel separation or retry logic
anchors.3: Per-channel queues, workers with retry, preference service, rate limiter
anchors.4: Priority-aware queuing, graceful provider failover, batch processing pipeline, clean separation

anchors.1: No concrete implementation for reliability or delivery guarantees
anchors.2: Basic retry without DLQ or deduplication
anchors.3: Retry with DLQ, deduplication via idempotency keys, provider failover
anchors.4: Comprehensive reliability design with metrics, alerting, circuit breaker for providers, and digest batching

checklist: Per-channel queues vs single queue (isolation vs complexity)
Push vs pull for in-app notifications
Immediate vs batched delivery (user experience vs cost)

anchors.1: No trade-off awareness in the design
anchors.3: Analyzed 2-3 trade-offs with reasoning for each choice
anchors.4: Systematic trade-off analysis including cost implications and user experience impact

commonMistakes: Synchronous delivery to channel providers (no queuing)
Not checking user preferences before sending
Single queue for all channels (one slow provider blocks all)
Not handling provider rate limits (e.g., SMS providers throttle)
Ignoring notification fatigue (too many notifications drive users away)
